narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some academic areas, like your Year 1 school decile ranking of 6, which is slightly better than average. However, your Year 3 decile of 8 and middle family income bracket placed you in a more typical range compared to other students in the dataset. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 70% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the school. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 32, well below the average of 36.7, which carried the strongest weight in signaling potential challenges ahead. Next in importance, your Year 3 decile ranking worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing by showing stronger relative performance later in school compared to peers. Your middle family income also nudged the odds toward failure, as it aligns with profiles where students sometimes face added pressures that impact bar success. Overall, while your full-time student status is the norm for 93% of applicants and didn't sway things much, the LSAT's dominant negative pull outweighed these positives, setting you apart from students with higher scores who typically see approval. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening recent academic deciles through grad school or additional coursework might also help counterbalance it. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback on bolstering your profile, and explore programs with holistic reviews that value your above-average undergrad GPA. With focused effort, you can reapply stronger next cycle.
